commentaries.author: Braulio of Zaragoza
commentaries.content: It happened one day that the enemy of the human race met the wrestler of the eternal King on a journey and challenged him with these words: “If you would like to see what each of us can accomplish with his strength, let us have a contest.” Barely finished speaking, he approached the saint and touched him in visible and corporeal reality, and for some time tried his wavering opponent, but the latter pressed Christ with prayers, and the divine aid strengthened his trembling steps and straightway caused the fugitive, apostate spirit to vanish into air. If it seems incredible to anyone that an invisible spirit can become substantial, save in the mystical sense, let it be explained to him how the divine pages narrate the struggle of Jacob with the angel too. I have this to say: that it would require less boldness for Satan to tempt a servant than the Lord, than Christ, man than God, the creature than the Creator. Life of St.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content:
Wept. Septuagint, "they wept, and besought me. "Other interpreters agree with the Vulgate.
Us. By changing a vowel point, in Hebrew, it might be, "He spoke to him. "(Cap. Grotius)
The most magnificent promises were made, at Bethel, regarding the Israelites: this made the profanation of the place more horrible. (Calmet)
Septuagint, "They found me in the house of On, and there the word was addressed to them. "
Bet haven was the name of Bethel, among the pious Jews, in the days of Osee. (Haydock)

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content: There were places in Jerusalem where he taught and other places to which he retired. “In daytime he was teaching in the temple.” Just this had been foretold by Hosea: “In my house did they find me, and there did I speak with them.”
